title: Blessed Among Women
content:
To the Signora Cairoli




آ آ آ آ Blessed was she that bare,
آ آ آ آ Hidden in flesh most fair,
آ آ آ آ For all menï؟½s sake the likeness of all love;
آ آ آ آ Holy that virginï؟½s womb,
آ آ آ آ The old record saith, on whom
آ آ آ آ The glory of God alighted as a dove;
آ آ آ آ Blessed, who brought to gracious birth
آ آ آ آ The sweet-souled Saviour of a man-tormented earth.



آ آ آ آ But four times art thou blest,
آ آ آ آ At whose most holy breast
آ آ آ آ Four times a godlike soldier-saviour hung;
آ آ آ آ And thence a fourfold Christ
آ آ آ آ Given to be sacrificed
آ آ آ آ To the same cross as the same bosom clung;
آ آ آ آ Poured the same blood, to leave the same
آ آ آ آ Light on the many-folded mountain-skirts of fame.



آ آ آ آ Shall they and thou not live,
آ آ آ آ The children thou didst give
آ آ آ آ Forth of thine hands, a godlike gift, to death,
آ آ آ آ Through fire of death to pass
آ آ آ آ For her high sake that was
آ آ آ آ Thine and their mother, that gave all you breath?
آ آ آ آ Shall ye not live till time drop dead,
آ آ آ آ O mother, and each her childrenï؟½s consecrated head?



آ آ آ آ Many brought gifts to take
آ آ آ آ For her loveï؟½s supreme sake,
آ آ آ آ Life and lifeï؟½s love, pleasure and praise and rest,
آ آ آ آ And went forth bare; but thou,
آ آ آ آ So much once richer, and now
آ آ آ آ Poorer than all these, more than these be blest;
آ آ آ آ Poorer so much, by so much given,
آ آ آ آ Than who gives earth for heavenï؟½s sake, not for earthï؟½s sake heaven.



آ آ آ آ Somewhat could each soul save,
آ آ آ آ What thing soever it gave,
آ آ آ آ But thine, mother, what has thy soul kept back?
آ آ آ آ None of thine all, not one,
آ آ آ آ To serve thee and be thy son,
آ آ آ آ Feed with love all thy days, lest one day lack;
آ آ آ آ All thy whole lifeï؟½s love, thine heartï؟½s whole,
آ آ آ آ Thou hast given as who gives gladly, O thou the supreme soul.



آ آ آ آ The heartï؟½s pure flesh and blood,
آ آ آ آ The heaven thy motherhood,
آ آ آ آ The live lips, the live eyes, that lived on thee;
آ آ آ آ The hands that clove with sweet
آ آ آ آ Blind clutch to thine, the feet
آ آ آ آ That felt on earth their first way to thy knee;
آ آ آ آ The little laughter of mouths milk-fed,
آ آ آ آ Now open again to feed on dust among the dead;



آ آ آ آ The fair, strong, young menï؟½s strength,
آ آ آ آ Light of life-days and length,
آ آ آ آ And glory of earth seen under and stars above,
آ آ آ آ And years that bring to tame
آ آ آ آ Now the wild falcon fame,
آ آ آ آ Now, to stroke smooth, the dove-white breast of love;
آ آ آ آ The life unlived, the unsown seeds,
آ آ آ آ Suns unbeholden, songs unsung, and undone deeds.



آ آ آ آ Therefore shall manï؟½s love be
آ آ آ آ As an own son to thee,
آ آ آ آ And the worldï؟½s worship of thee for a child;
آ آ آ آ All thine own land as one
آ آ آ آ New-born, a nursing son,
آ آ آ آ All thine own people a new birth undefiled;
آ آ آ آ And all the unborn Italian time,
آ آ آ آ And all its glory, and all its works, thy seed sublime.



آ آ آ آ That henceforth no manï؟½s breath,
آ آ آ آ Saying ï؟½Italy,ï؟½ but saith
آ آ آ آ In that most sovereign word thine equal name;
آ آ آ آ Nor can one speak of thee
آ آ آ آ But he saith ï؟½Italy,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Seeing in two suns one co-eternal flame;
آ آ آ آ One heat, one heaven, one heart, one fire,
آ آ آ آ One light, one love, one benediction, one desire.



آ آ آ آ Blest above praise and prayer
آ آ آ آ And incense of menï؟½s air,
آ آ آ آ Thy place is higher than where such voices rise
آ آ آ آ As in menï؟½s temples make
آ آ آ آ Music for some vain sake,
آ آ آ آ This Godï؟½s or that Godï؟½s, in one weary wise;
آ آ آ آ Thee the soul silent, the shut heart,
آ آ آ آ The locked lips of the spirit praise thee that thou art.



آ آ آ آ Yea, for manï؟½s whole lifeï؟½s length,
آ آ آ آ And with manï؟½s whole soulï؟½s strength,
آ آ آ آ We praise thee, O holy, and bless thee, O mother of lights;
آ آ آ آ And send forth as on wings
آ آ آ آ The worldï؟½s heartï؟½s thanksgivings,
آ آ آ آ Song-birds to sing thy days through and thy nights;
آ آ آ آ And wrap thee around and arch thee above
آ آ آ آ With the air of benediction and the heaven of love.



آ آ آ آ And toward thee our unbreathed words
آ آ آ آ Fly speechless, winged as birds,
آ آ آ آ As the Indian flock, children of Paradise,
آ آ آ آ The winged things without feet,
آ آ آ آ Fed with Godï؟½s dew for meat,
آ آ آ آ That live in the air and light of the utter skies;
آ آ آ آ So fleet, so flying a footless flight,
آ آ آ آ With wings for feet love seeks thee, to partake thy sight.



آ آ آ آ Love like a clear sky spread
آ آ آ آ Bends over thy loved head,
آ آ آ آ As a new heaven bends over a new-born earth,
آ آ آ آ When the old nightï؟½s womb is great
آ آ آ آ With young stars passionate
آ آ آ آ And fair new planets fiery-fresh from birth;
آ آ آ آ And moon-white here, there hot like Mars,
آ آ آ آ Souls that are worlds shine on thee, spirits that are stars.



آ آ آ آ Till the whole sky burns through
آ آ آ آ With heavenï؟½s own heart-deep hue,
آ آ آ آ With passion-coloured glories of lit souls;
آ آ آ آ And thine above all names
آ آ آ آ Writ highest with lettering flames
آ آ آ آ Lightens, and all the old starriest aureoles
آ آ آ آ And all the old holiest memories wane,
آ آ آ آ And the old names of loveï؟½s chosen, found in thy sight vain.



آ آ آ آ And crowned heads are discrowned,
آ آ آ آ And stars sink without sound,
آ آ آ آ And loveï؟½s self for thy loveï؟½s sake waxes pale
آ آ آ آ Seeing from his storied skies
آ آ آ آ In what new reverent wise
آ آ آ آ Thee Romeï؟½s most highest, her sovereign daughters, hail;
آ آ آ آ Thee Portia, thee Veturia grey,
آ آ آ آ Thee Arria, thee Cornelia, Roman more than they.



آ آ آ آ Even all these as all we
آ آ آ آ Subdue themselves to thee,
آ آ آ آ Bow their heads haloed, quench their fiery fame;
آ آ آ آ Seen through dim years divine,
آ آ آ آ Their faint lights feminine
آ آ آ آ Sink, then spring up rekindled from thy flame;
آ آ آ آ Fade, then reflower and reillume
آ آ آ آ From thy fresh spring their wintering age with new-blown bloom.



آ آ آ آ To thy much holier head
آ آ آ آ Even theirs, the holy and dead,
آ آ آ آ Bow themselves each one from her heavenward height;
آ آ آ آ Each in her shining turn,
آ آ آ آ All tremble toward thee and yearn
آ آ آ آ To melt in thine their consummated light;
آ آ آ آ Till from dayï؟½s Capitolian dome
آ آ آ آ One glory of many glories lighten upon Rome.



آ آ آ آ Hush thyself, song, and cease,
آ آ آ آ Close, lips, and hold your peace;
آ آ آ آ What help hast thou, what part have ye herein?
آ آ آ آ But you, with sweet shut eyes,
آ آ آ آ Heart-hidden memories,
آ آ آ آ Dreams and dumb thoughts that keep what things have been
آ آ آ آ Silent, and pure of all words said,
آ آ آ آ Praise without song the living, without dirge the dead.



آ آ آ آ Thou, strengthless in these things,
آ آ آ آ Song, fold thy feebler wings,
آ آ آ آ And as a pilgrim go forth girt and shod,
آ آ آ آ And where the new graves are,
آ آ آ آ And where the sunset star,
آ آ آ آ To the pure spirit of man that men call God,
آ آ آ آ To the high soul of things, that is
آ آ آ آ Made of menï؟½s heavenlier hopes and mightier memories;



آ آ آ آ To the elements that make
آ آ آ آ For the soulï؟½s living sake
آ آ آ آ This raiment of dead things, of shadow and trance,
آ آ آ آ That give us chance and time
آ آ آ آ Wherein to aspire and climb
آ آ آ آ And set our lifeï؟½s work higher than time or chance
آ آ آ آ The old sacred elements, that give
آ آ آ آ The breath of life to days that die, to deeds that live;



آ آ آ آ To them, veiled gods and great,
آ آ آ آ There bow thee and dedicate
آ آ آ آ The speechless spirit in these thy weak words hidden;
آ آ آ آ And mix thy reverent breath
آ آ آ آ With holier air of death,
آ آ آ آ At the high feast of sorrow a guest unbidden,
آ آ آ آ Till with divine triumphal tears
آ آ آ آ Thou fill menï؟½s eyes who listen with a heart that hears.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Algernon Charles Swinburne represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Algernon Charles Swinburne's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
